content:
5 min read
Just now

--

Why “reading for longer” beats “searching harder.”
If you’ve built RAG for anything mission-critical — legal, compliance, insurance — you know the pattern: the demo shines, then production accuracy collapses on the questions that matter. Here’s why, and a fix called Chain-of-Memory retrieval.
Why the standard approaches fail
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Vector RAG can’t follow multi-hop reasoning. Ask “Why did coastal claim payouts spike?” when the real chain is endorsement lowered the deductible more claims eligible → payouts spiked — those embeddings live in different neighborhoods, so nearest-neighbor search never connects them. Add hard-to-debug embeddings and a dozen fragile tuning knobs, and failures become impossible to diagnose.
Long-context LLMs beat vector RAG on benchmarks, but attention is O(n²) (pre-filling 128k tokens ≈ 3 minutes on a quantized 70B), accuracy visibly drops past ~40k tokens, real document sets blow past 200k, and — worst for compliance — the model can fabricate quotes it claims to have retrieved.
Both hallucinate, just differently: vector RAG from missing context, long-context from inventing it.
Read like a human expert Chain-of-Memory: reading for longer
Chain-of-Thought made models better by letting them think for longer. Chain-of-Memory applies the same idea to retrieval: let the model read for longer, one manageable chunk at a time, while maintaining a running “memory” — exactly like a human expert taking notes while reading a long contract clause by clause.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

small models for the token-heavy reading, a strong reasoning model only for the short final step.
1.Plan first
PLANNING_PROMPT = “””
User query: {query}
Document summary: {doc_summary}
As a checklist:
1. What key terms need defining?
2. What specific information is needed?
3. What assumptions should we avoid?
“””
2. Read with memory
Press enter or click to view image in full size

def chain_of_memory(query, plan, chunks, retriever_llm):
memory = “No notes yet.”
for chunk in chunks:
memory = retriever_llm.generate(RETRIEVAL_PROMPT.format(
query=query, plan=plan, memory=memory, chunk=chunk))
return memory # compact, evidence-grounded notes
The prompt instructs: extract evidence, don’t conclude; keep relevant notes, add new facts with sentence IDs, and revise anything the new chunk contradicts. That last part is the superpower — when the reader hits Endorsement W-14 in chunk 9, it revises the deductible it noted from the base policy in chunk 2. Independent top-K retrieval can never do that.
Need speed instead of sequence? Run chunks in parallel (with precomputed KV caches, N chunks finish in roughly the latency of one), or use a cheap triage pass to discard irrelevant chunks before careful reading. It’s a dial on the throughput–accuracy curve.
3. Retrieve IDs, not text — hallucinations become impossible
Every sentence, table, and figure gets an ID during preprocessing. The retriever outputs IDs plus reasoning, never verbatim quotes:
retrieval_output = {
“evidence_ids”: [“s_1100”, “s_1200”],
“reasoning”: “s_1100 confirms burst-pipe coverage; s_1200 is an “
“endorsement overriding the base deductible.”,
}
# resolve: only IDs that exist in the store return text
evidence = [store[i] for i in retrieval_output[“evidence_ids”] if i in store]
An invented ID resolves to nothing, so fake quotes are structurally impossible. Bonus: fewer output tokens, and the reasoning + metadata doubles as an audit trail — far better UX than top-K’s random text blobs.
4. Synthesize with a strong model
The evidence is now short, so a top-tier reasoning model answers cheaply and fast .One more speed trick: structure chunk prompts so the document is a fixed prefix, compute its KV cache once, and persist it to CPU/disk. Hosted APIs cache prompts for minutes; self-hosted models keep caches indefinitely — cutting time-to-first-token from tens of seconds to 1–2 seconds.
Implementing it on GCP with Gemini
Don’t want to self-host? Gemini’s context caching is the managed equivalent of KV-cache hot-swapping: upload the document once, Google stores the processed context server-side, and every follow-up question references it by cache name — cached tokens are billed at a steep discount, and you skip re-sending 100k+ tokens per query.
The model mapping is natural: Gemini Flash (fast, cheap) for planning and retrieval, Gemini Pro (strong reasoning) for synthesis only.
from google import genai
from google.genai import types
# Vertex AI (uses your GCP project + IAM)
client = genai.Client(vertexai=True, project=”my-project”, location=”us-central1")
# or the Gemini API directly: client = genai.Client(api_key=API_KEY)
RETRIEVER = “gemini-2.5-flash”
REASONER = “gemini-2.5-pro”
# — — — — — Preprocessing: one explicit cache per chunk — — — — —
def warm_chunk_caches(chunks: list[str]) -> list[str]:
cache_names = []
for i, chunk in enumerate(chunks):
cache = client.caches.create(
model=RETRIEVER,
config=types.CreateCachedContentConfig(
display_name=f”policy_doc_chunk_{i}”,
system_instruction=(
“Extract evidence relevant to the question. Do NOT “
“draw conclusions. Cite sentence IDs. Revise notes “
“if this chunk overrides earlier facts.”
),
contents=[chunk],
ttl=”86400s”,
),
)
cache_names.append(cache.name)
return cache_names
# — — — — — Query time: retrieval hits the cache — — — — —
def retrieve_from_chunk(cache_name, query, plan, memory):
resp = client.models.generate_content(
model=RETRIEVER,
contents=f”Question: {query}\nPlan: {plan}\nNotes so far: {memory}”,
config=types.GenerateContentConfig(cached_content=cache_name),
)
return resp.text
####sequential; parallelize with asyncio####
def chain_of_memory_gemini(query, plan, cache_names):
memory = “No notes yet.”
for name in cache_names:
memory = retrieve_from_chunk(name, query, plan, memory)
return memory
# — — — — — Synthesis: short context, strong model — — — — —
def answer(query: str, evidence: str) -> str:
resp = client.models.generate_content(
model=REASONER,
contents=f”Evidence:\n{evidence}\n\nUsing only this evidence, “
f”answer: {query}”,
)
return resp.text
A few production notes:
Takeaways

• Plan → read-with-memory → synthesize, with a right-sized model per stage.

• Sequential memory lets later evidence revise earlier notes — critical where endorsements or amendments override the main text.

• Retrieve by sentence ID: hallucinated evidence becomes impossible by construction, and every answer is auditable.

• Persist KV caches (or Gemini context caches) so repeated Q&A over the same corpus is nearly instant.
Accuracy in document Q&A is a systems problem, not a model problem. Structure the reading the way a careful human would, and smaller models beat brute force.
